narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite challenges like your low family income bracket, and we appreciate the courage it takes to get this far. The admissions model evaluated your profile by predicting your likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, and it estimated a 42% chance that you might not—high enough to lead to the tough decision of rejecting your application right now. This prediction was driven mostly by a few key parts of your background compared to typical students. Your family income, which is in the low bracket well below the average, actually worked strongly in your favor, pulling the prediction toward expecting you to pass, perhaps recognizing the resilience that often comes with overcoming financial hurdles. On the other hand, your Year 1 school decile ranking of 3—lower than the typical 5.71—pushed the model toward predicting failure, as it signaled early academic struggles relative to peers. Similarly, your LSAT score of 29, much lower than the average 36.7, contributed toward that concerning outcome by highlighting a gap in standardized test performance. Your Year 3 decile of 4, also below the usual 5.53, had a milder positive pull toward passing, while your undergraduate GPA of 2.9 edged slightly in your favor despite being under the 3.21 average, and your full-time student status aligned with the vast majority and offered a small boost. Overall, these factors placed you below the threshold where the model sees strong bar passage odds for most applicants. It's disheartening, but this isn't the end—consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, strengthening your later-year grades, or seeking programs with extra support for students from similar backgrounds. You have the grit to improve these areas and reapply stronger; many have turned predictions like this around. We're rooting for you to succeed on your path to becoming a lawyer.
